nativeRange.habitat: The Palmas Bichir inhabits slow-moving or still freshwater environments across West Africa, including rivers, streams, floodplain pools, and marshy marginal areas. It is typically found over soft substrate with abundant cover, and frequently occurs in low-oxygen or turbid water where its ability to breathe air directly from the surface gives it a considerable advantage.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — coarse gravel abrades the belly and pectoral fins)
smooth driftwood pieces and rounded rocks arranged into caves and overhangs for daytime refuge
robust or artificial plants for additional cover and low-light diffusion
dim or diffused lighting suited to nocturnal habits
gentle filtration — strong surface turbulence impedes air-breathing access
fully sealed lid with no gaps: all cable and pipe entry points blocked with foam or mesh — confirmed escape artist

diet.notes: Obligate carnivore hunting primarily by olfaction; eyesight is poor. Offer meaty live or frozen foods: earthworms, prawns, mussel, lancefish, bloodworms, and chunky frozen carnivore mixes. Most specimens reject dried foods initially; some can be weaned onto quality sinking carnivore pellets with persistence. Feed after lights-out (2–3 times per week for adults); target feeding with tongs is advisable in community tanks to prevent food theft by faster-sighted tankmates. Remove uneaten food promptly to avoid ammonia spikes.

goodWith: other Polypterus species of closely matched size (P. senegalus, P. delhezi, P. ornatipinnis in adequately sized tanks)
large peaceful cichlids that do not target the bichir’s belly
avoidWith: any fish small enough to fit in its mouth — will be consumed overnight
small tetras, danios, rasboras, and other nano fish
fin-nipping species that may attack the dorsal finlets or soft belly
aggressive cichlids that bite at the gill region
very fast or boisterous fish that outcompete it for food

imageCandidates.notes: John P. Friel; 1024x768. Sharp full lateral view of the whole fish showing the pale olive-yellow body with fine dark marbled barring, dorsal finlets and slightly protruding lower jaw diagnostic of P. palmas. Minor glass reflection at top but subject crisp.

John P. Friel; 1024x768. Sharp head/foreparts close-up clearly showing the evenly-meeting/protruding lower jaw and marbled head speckling; framed as a feature crop (tail out of frame), kept as supporting detail.

Lymantria; 1976x850. Different individual in a planted aquarium showing the leopard/marbled dark pattern in a natural setting; slightly soft with a green cast so kept as non-recommended variety filler.

summary: The Palmas Bichir (Polypterus palmas) is a smaller West African bichir, typically growing to around 28–35 cm, and is well suited to intermediate fishkeepers. Like all bichirs, it breathes air directly from the water surface and must have unobstructed access to it at all times. It is nocturnal, carnivorous, and a confirmed escape artist that requires a fully sealed aquarium lid.
careNotes:
The Palmas Bichir belongs to a group within the genus Polypterus in which the lower jaw protrudes noticeably beyond the upper, or the jaws meet roughly evenly — a feature that distinguishes it from larger relatives such as Polypterus endlicherii and P. ornatipinnis, where the upper jaw protrudes instead. Closely related species in the same grouping include P. senegalus and P. delhezi. The type locality (the location where the species was first formally described) is Cape Palmas in Liberia, which gives the fish its common name. Two subspecies are recognised: P. p. palmas from southern populations, which shows a pale yellow body with thin dark barring, and P. p. buettikoferi (sometimes sold as the Gold Dust Bichir) from northern populations, which displays deeper gold colouring with heavier barring. Both may be encountered in the trade under the shared common name Palmas Bichir.

OBLIGATE AIR-BREATHER: Like all Polypteridae, P. palmas possesses a divided, lung-like swim bladder and must have constant, unobstructed access to the water surface; any obstruction risks asphyxiation. Equally, the tank must be fully sealed — this species can escape through surprisingly small gaps, survive briefly on damp surfaces, and has been discovered across rooms by morning. All cable and pipe entry points must be blocked with filter foam or mesh.

TANK REQUIREMENTS: A minimum of 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is recommended for a single adult. Fine sand substrate is essential — coarse gravel causes chronic abrasion ulcers on the belly and pectoral fins. Decor should provide substantial daytime hiding (driftwood caves, smooth rocks), as this species is strictly nocturnal and requires shelter to avoid chronic stress. Lighting should be dim or heavily diffused. Filtration must handle heavy organic waste from a protein-rich diet but should not create strong currents near the bichir’s resting area.

FEEDING: P. palmas hunts by olfaction rather than vision. In a community setting, faster-sighted tankmates will intercept food before the bichir locates it; target feeding with tongs or a feeding wand after lights-out is strongly recommended. Most specimens initially reject dried foods but can be conditioned onto sinking carnivore pellets over time, which reduces biosecurity risks associated with live feeder fish.

COMPATIBILITY: Any tankmate small enough to fit in the bichir’s mouth gape will eventually be consumed, typically at night. At 28–35 cm this still covers many medium hobby fish. Conspecific keeping is possible in adequately sized tanks with size-matched individuals. Unlike P. endlicherii, this species is tractable enough for a 200 L+ intermediate setup without a specialist-only advisory.

WATER QUALITY: Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are essential. Ammonia and nitrite must be maintained at zero; nitrates below 20 ppm. This species is copper-sensitive and should not be treated with copper-based medications. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.0, gH 5–15) mimics the blackwater West African biotope and is preferable for long-term health.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria; most specimens in trade are wild-caught. Spawning is triggered by rainy-season simulation: a period of slightly cooler, softer water followed by large warm water changes and high-protein conditioning feeds. The male wraps his anal fin (which is broader than the female’s) around the female’s vent and externally fertilises 100–300 adhesive eggs as they are released; eggs are scattered among fine-leafed plants or spawning mops. Parents consume eggs if not removed promptly. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 27°C; larvae bear external gills that are gradually reabsorbed. Fry require live micro-foods (baby brine shrimp, micro-worms) initially and must be size-sorted frequently to prevent sibling predation.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Polypterus palmas palmas
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 32.5 cm / 13 in), temperature 25–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 5–15 dH, minimum tank 120 × 45 × 37.5 cm (~200 L), native range (Ghana, DRC, Cameroon, Liberia, Guinea, Côte d’Ivoire, Sierra Leone), diet (prawns, earthworms, mussel, lancefish), nocturnal olfactory hunting, escape risk, obligate air-breather status, lower-jaw clade classification, compatibility notes, and breeding remarks.

additional.name: FishBase — Polypterus palmas
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 35.3 cm SL), taxonomy (Polypteridae, Polypteriformes, Cladistii), native range (Guinea Bissau, Liberia, Côte d’Ivoire, Senegal, Casamance River), trophic level (2.9, carnivore), facultative/obligate air-breathing classification, and freshwater demersal habitat.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Polypterus palmas
additional.notes: Confirmed two recognized subspecies (P. p. palmas and P. p. buettikoferi), type locality at Cape Palmas (Liberia), typical aquarium size around 30 cm with 35.3 cm maximum, West African range across Ghana, DRC, Cameroon, Liberia, Guinea, Côte d’Ivoire, Sierra Leone, IUCN Least Concern (2021), nocturnal lateral-line hunting, all trade specimens wild-caught, and lifespan 10–20 years.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Marbled Bichir (Polypterus palmas)
additional.notes: Hobbyist trade source. Confirmed adult size up to 14 inches (35.6 cm), 74–82°F temperature, pH 6.2–7.5, 120-gallon minimum tank, nocturnal feeding schedule (feed before lights-out), lifespan 10–15 years typical up to 20 years, breeding difficulty (100–300 eggs, parents cannibalize eggs), escape risk, and compatibility with Oscars, Jack Dempseys, Flowerhorn Cichlids, and similar large tankmates.

additional.name: Biotope Aquarium Project — Polypterus palmas
additional.notes: Biotope-oriented profile confirming blackwater West African creek habitat (slow current, soft substrate), adult size 30 cm SL, temperature 25–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, gH 5–15, meaty diet (prawns, earthworms, mussel, lancefish), monogamous substrate spawning, 3–4 day incubation, and confirmed escape-artist behavior requiring tight-fitting lid.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved and most trade specimens are wild-caught. Spawning is believed to be triggered by a rainy-season simulation involving a temperature drop, softer water, and large warm water changes. The male wraps his broader anal fin around the female's vent to carry out external fertilisation, scattering 100–300 adhesive eggs among plants. Parents should be removed immediately after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days, and the larvae initially bear external gills. Fry should be size-sorted frequently to prevent sibling predation.
